key_insight: The paper replaces hard, discrete topology constraints with differentiable Hodge-spectral surrogates that target zero and near-zero Laplacian modes to optimize topology-aware objectives.
engineering_relevance: This gives a practical way to impose homological structure in optimization for point clouds and graphs while keeping gradients smooth enough for modern learning and design workflows.
method: Build soft graph/clique-complex and point-cloud Hodge-Laplacian relaxations that converge to the ordinary Hodge Laplacian in the hard limit.
Use low-pass spectral filters—heat, resolvent, and polynomial moment losses—to represent Betti-like information differentiably.
Optimize topology-constrained objectives with geometry-aware gradients that unify graph clique complexes and Vietoris-Rips filtrations.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that Hodge spectral-filter losses produce more spatially distributed gradients, smoother scale-normalized behavior under persistence-pairing changes, and geometry-aware update directions than persistent-homology losses.
watch_item: The approach relies on spectral relaxations and penalty choices, so numerical stability and fidelity to exact homology can depend on the softness of the relaxation and filter design.
paper_title: Hodge Spectral Surrogates for Topology-Constrained Optimization
